glossary.sentence: To update the weights of the output neurons, the following equation is used: In the hidden layers, the error for each processing unit is calculated in the following way: To update the weights of the hidden neurons, the following equation is used:

glossary.definition: The storage area in an expert system where knowledge is stored.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: The component of an expert system that processes the knowledge in the knowledge base to make decisions or provide explanations.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: The method used to describe knowledge that the inference engine relies on.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine depends on a knowledge representation schema that is used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: A collection of unrelated facts, such as purchase transactions or student grades, stored separately from a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that emulates the human expert’s methodology and performance (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Expert systems address a variety of problems: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data. Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations. Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms. Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints. Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints. Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains). Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Expert systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data. Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations. Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms. Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints. Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints. Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains). Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: Means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983). Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created. Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them. They are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology (Luger, 2009). These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc. All the mentioned concepts define the meaning of the concept “university”. Research works in psychology have revealed that concepts are not only related by associations but they are also organized in a hierarchical manner.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them. Moreover, networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc. All the mentioned concepts define the meaning of the concept “university”. Research works in psychology have revealed that concepts are not only related by associations but they are also organized in a hierarchical manner.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992). Therefore, a knowledge base rooted in semantic networks includes a network of concepts and their relations. Graphically semantic networks are represented using only two symbols.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992). Therefore, a knowledge base rooted in semantic networks includes a network of concepts and their relations. Graphically semantic networks are represented using only two symbols.

glossary.definition: Represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts. A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties. The direction of the relation is from a narrower concept to a broader concept.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts. A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties. The direction of the relation is from a narrower concept to a broader concept.

glossary.definition: Represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.

glossary.definition: An experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge. They asked people about birds and their features and recorded reaction time. The mean reaction time changed in relation to the complexity of a sentence. This proved that knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels and time is needed to go along the path from the activated concept to its features.
glossary.sentence: Collins and Quillian made an experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge. They asked people about birds and their features and recorded reaction time. The mean reaction time changed in relation to the complexity of a sentence. This proved that knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels and time is needed to go along the path from the activated concept to its features.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'Part of' (Fig.8) that allows relating parts of an object to the whole object, with the direction of the relation from parts to whole objects.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'property/has a property of' (Fig.9) that allows relating objects/concepts to their properties, with the direction of the relation from the object to a property.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title 'value' (Fig.10) that allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values, with the direction of the relation from the property to its value.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10).

glossary.definition: Relations that allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: A mechanism in which subclasses inherit slots from their superclass.
glossary.sentence: They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added: Affectionate in the Cat sub-class and Roar and Barking in the Dog sub-class.

glossary.definition: Structured ways to represent and understand knowledge, allowing ease of representation and understanding, defining a variety of relations between objects/concepts, representing knowledge in a structured way, supporting inheritance, and having a simple inference engine; they have disadvantages such as representing only declarative knowledge and introducing ambiguity.
glossary.sentence: Semantic networks have a number of advantages: they allow ease of representation and understanding of knowledge; they allow defining a variety of relations between objects/ concepts; they represent knowledge in a structured way; they support inheritance; they have a simple inference engine. At the same time, semantic networks have disadvantages: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts ); they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways; they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

glossary.definition: An example of structured knowledge representation schemas developed by Marvin Minsky in 1975, based on the idea of adapting a remembered framework to fit reality by changing details as necessary.
glossary.sentence: Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975. They are based on the following idea: “When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” (Minsky, 1975)

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge, where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts, and relations are represented by referencing principles.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Moreover, relations are not represented explicitly by arcs at it was done in semantic networks, but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name.

glossary.definition: A structured representation of knowledge about an object or concept. It contains slots (attributes) with values associated with a particular instance or class.
glossary.sentence: The frame Card#2967 contains the calculated value for the slot Ensured services “Luggage insurance for free ” ; ... The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: An attribute or property of a frame that stores a value associated with an object or concept.
glossary.sentence: They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added: Affectionate in the Cat sub-class and Roar and Barking in the Dog sub-class.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames in a frame-based expert system that represents extended knowledge about slot values and tells the inference engine how to process the property of the object (Negnevitsky, 2011). Facets can contain value type, constraints, actions, etc.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value (Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A source from which frame-based expert systems can provide slot values, where many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Different sources can provide slot values: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base; external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values; procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text ); user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation; inheritance – frames support inheritance of slots and their values (see further in the text).

glossary.definition: Programs that intelligent systems can communicate with to deliver slot values for a frame-based expert system, such as databases or expert systems (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Different sources can provide slot values: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base; external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values; procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text ); user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation; inheritance – frames support inheritance of slots and their values (see further in the text).

glossary.definition: Procedures and methods assigned to slots in a frame-based expert system that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Different sources can provide slot values: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base; external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values; procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text ); user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation; inheritance – frames support inheritance of slots and their values (see further in the text).

glossary.definition: Slot values provided by users during the operation of a frame-based expert system (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Different sources can provide slot values: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base; external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values; procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text ); user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation; inheritance – frames support inheritance of slots and their values (see further in the text).

glossary.definition: A relationship between a superclass and its subclasses, where subclasses inherit attributes from the superclass.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal .

glossary.definition: A relationship between two frames in a frame-based expert system that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: An aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.

glossary.definition: A relationship between two frames in a frame-based expert system that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: An association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A method used to relate two frames in a frame-based expert system where a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system that can be assigned to slots in a frame-based expert system for various purposes (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons. A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system (Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: A procedure or routine that can be associated with a slot in a frame to perform specific actions when the slot's value is accessed or modified.
glossary.sentence: The inferer IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: A type of frame in a frame-based expert system that can have slots and values inherited by sub-class frames (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Different sources can provide slot values: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base; external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values; procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text ); user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation; inheritance – frames support inheritance of slots and their values (see further in the text).

glossary.definition: A type of frame in a frame-based expert system that can inherit slots and values from class frames but can also contain unique properties (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems (Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A type of frame in a frame-based expert system that can inherit slots and values from class frames but can also contain unique properties (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems (Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A specific realization or example of a class frame.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: A relationship between frames that indicates a connection or association between objects.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system where domain knowledge is represented as a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: The most popular expert systems are rule-based systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A rule in the knowledge base that consists of an IF part (conditions) and a THEN part (actions), used by the inference engine to relate facts and reach conclusions.
glossary.sentence: the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF or THEN part (it depends on the search direction) of all rules available in the knowledge base

glossary.definition: A rule-based expert system consisting of a knowledge base with production rules, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A storage area where facts with time tags are kept and used for conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory...

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern.

glossary.definition: Data relevant to a specific problem or case in a rule-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: The basis of the inference mechanism that implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between working memory content and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: Mechanisms within an expert system that provide users with information about how a particular conclusion was reached, including the line of reasoning and validity.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The process of selecting one rule from a set of enabled rules (a conflict set) to fire and change the content of working memory.
glossary.sentence: For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction )

glossary.definition: The method used for choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire...

glossary.definition: A component intended for editing rules in the knowledge base, including adding new rules and modifying or deleting existing ones.
glossary.sentence: The developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The action of executing a rule by adding its THEN part to the working memory (in data-driven search) or its IF part to the working memory (in goal-driven search).
glossary.sentence: firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues

glossary.definition: Methods used to make the search process more effective in a rule-based expert system. Three types are mentioned: control through the selection of search direction, control through the selection of rule sequence and structure, and control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: An illustration of a specific sequence of things to try, suggesting a particular order for applying rules in a problem-solving context.
glossary.sentence: For example, when a mechanic says, “if the engine does not turn over and the lights don’t come on, then check the battery” , he/she suggests a specific sequence of things to try. This form of the rules is critical in controlling search, making the system behave logically.

glossary.definition: Statements of the form IF condition THEN action or conclusion that represent knowledge in a rule-based system.
glossary.sentence: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy. Thinking about the structure of production rules...

glossary.definition: A logical operator that introduces ambiguity when used in production rules.
glossary.sentence: try avoiding the use of OR in production rules because this operator introduces ambiguity;

glossary.definition: A logical operator that should not be combined with OR in the same rule to avoid ambiguity.
glossary.sentence: if it is not possible to avoid using OR, try avoiding the use of combinations of AND and OR in the same rule;

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which a single rule is chosen to fire.
glossary.sentence: known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution strategy that fires the first or last rule found in the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: to fire the first/the last rule found. In this scheme, the conflict set is not assembled at all...

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution strategy that selects the rule with the highest priority if multiple rules can fire.
glossary.sentence: to fire rules based on their priorities. Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value...

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution method that selects the most specific rule to fire.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption...

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution strategy that fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached...

glossary.definition: The property that rules have no syntactic interactions and may only affect other rules by changing the pattern in working memory.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic...

glossary.definition: The ability to trace the execution of production rules and provide meaningful explanations of the system’s current state and action.
glossary.sentence: Tracing and explanation. The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace...

glossary.definition: Knowledge that is not fully known or certain, which rule-based expert systems can represent and reason with using confidence factors.
glossary.sentence: Dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge. Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing...

glossary.definition: A value used in rule-based expert systems to represent the degree of certainty or confidence associated with a rule's conclusion.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: The ability to successively add, delete, or modify the knowledge (rules) of a system without affecting other parts of the system.
glossary.sentence: This syntactic independence supports the incremental development of expert systems by successively adding...
